title: Unit Economics Deep Dive
objective: Master the core unit economics metrics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, and payback period — and learn how to calculate them accurately using blended, segmented, and cohort-based approaches so you can diagnose whether your business model actually works.
beginnerExplanation:
Unit economics answer the most fundamental question about your business: does each customer you acquire generate more value than it costs to acquire them? If the answer is no, growth actually makes things worse — you lose more money with every new customer.

The first metric is Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC). This is the total cost of acquiring one new customer. To calculate it, take all your sales and marketing spend for a period and divide by the number of new customers acquired in that period. If you spent $10,000 on marketing and sales last month and acquired 20 new customers, your CAC is $500. Be honest about what goes into this number — include salaries, tools, ad spend, agency fees, and any other cost that exists to bring in new customers.

The second metric is Lifetime Value (LTV), sometimes called Customer Lifetime Value (CLV). This is the total gross profit you expect to earn from a customer over their entire relationship with you. The simplest formula is: Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) multiplied by Gross Margin multiplied by Average Customer Lifespan. If a customer pays $80 per month, your gross margin is 75%, and customers stay for an average of 18 months, the LTV is $80 x 0.75 x 18 = $1,080.

The third metric is the LTV:CAC ratio, which tells you how efficiently your business converts acquisition spend into long-term value. Divide LTV by CAC. In our example, $1,080 / $500 = 2.16x. The general benchmark is that a healthy LTV:CAC ratio is 3:1 or above. Below 1:1 means you are losing money on every customer. Between 1:1 and 3:1 means the model is marginal and needs improvement.

The fourth metric is payback period — how many months it takes to recover the cost of acquiring a customer. If your CAC is $500 and each customer generates $60 in gross profit per month ($80 ARPU x 75% margin), your payback period is $500 / $60 = 8.3 months. For most SaaS businesses, a payback period under 12 months is healthy. Longer than 18 months means you need significant capital to fund growth.

These four metrics together tell you whether your growth engine is sustainable or whether you are building on sand.
deeperExplanation:
Blended unit economics — where you average CAC and LTV across all customers — are useful as a starting point but can be dangerously misleading. The real power comes from segmenting and analysing by cohort.

Segmented unit economics means calculating CAC and LTV separately for different customer groups. Your enterprise customers acquired through outbound sales might have a CAC of $2,000 but an LTV of $15,000 (7.5x ratio), while your SMB customers acquired through paid search might have a CAC of $200 but an LTV of only $400 (2x ratio). Blended together, the ratio looks acceptable at 3.5x — but the SMB segment is barely viable, and you would not know this without segmenting.

Cohort analysis takes this further by tracking customers based on when they were acquired. The January 2025 cohort might retain at 85% after 6 months, while the July 2025 cohort retains at only 70%. If you only look at blended averages, you miss the fact that retention is declining — which means your LTV is shrinking and your model is deteriorating. Cohort-based LTV is the gold standard because it reflects actual customer behaviour rather than averages across time periods.

Payback period deserves deeper attention because it determines your capital requirements. A 12-month payback means you need to fund 12 months of operating costs before each customer becomes profitable. If you are acquiring 100 customers per month at $500 CAC, that is $50,000 per month in acquisition spend that takes a year to recover. At scale, this creates enormous working capital pressure.

Finally, the relationship between retention and LTV is non-linear. Improving monthly retention from 95% to 97% does not sound dramatic, but it extends average customer lifespan from 20 months to 33 months — a 65% increase in LTV from a 2-percentage-point improvement. This is why the best operators obsess over retention before acquisition.
keyTakeaways: CAC must include all sales and marketing costs — salaries, tools, ads, agencies — not just direct ad spend, otherwise you are understating your true acquisition cost.
LTV should be calculated on gross profit, not revenue — what matters is the margin you keep, not the total amount the customer pays.
Blended LTV:CAC ratios can mask segment-level problems — always segment by channel, customer type, or product tier to see the real picture.
Payback period determines your capital requirements — a long payback means you need more cash to fund growth, even if the LTV:CAC ratio is healthy.
Small improvements in retention create disproportionately large improvements in LTV — focus on keeping customers before spending more to acquire new ones.
example: A SaaS company is growing at 20% quarter-over-quarter and the founder is confident the model is working. The blended numbers: $500 CAC, $80/month ARPU, 18-month average lifespan, 75% gross margin. LTV = $80 x 0.75 x 18 = $1,080. LTV:CAC = $1,080 / $500 = 2.16x. Payback period = $500 / ($80 x 0.75) = 8.3 months. The 2.16x ratio is below the 3:1 benchmark — the model is marginal. When the founder segments by acquisition channel, the picture gets clearer: organic and referral customers have a CAC of $150 and LTV of $1,400 (9.3x ratio — excellent), but paid acquisition customers have a CAC of $800 and LTV of $900 (1.1x — barely break-even). The growth is being funded by pouring money into a paid channel that barely returns its cost. The diagnosis: either dramatically improve paid conversion rates, reduce paid CAC through better targeting, or reallocate budget toward scaling the organic and referral channels that actually work.
commonMistakes: Understating CAC by only counting ad spend and ignoring salaries, tools, agencies, and overhead — the true cost of acquisition is almost always higher than founders think.
Calculating LTV on revenue instead of gross profit — a customer paying $100/month with 50% margins generates $50/month in value, not $100, and ignoring this overstates your LTV by 2x.
Using only blended metrics without segmenting by channel, customer type, or cohort — blended averages hide the segments that are dragging your economics down.
Optimising for LTV:CAC ratio by cutting acquisition spend rather than improving the product — a high ratio from low spend just means you are not growing, which is not the same as being efficient.
exercise: Calculate your own unit economics right now. (1) List every cost that contributes to customer acquisition last month: ad spend, sales salaries, marketing tools, agency fees, content production. Total these up. (2) Count how many new customers you acquired last month. (3) Calculate your CAC: total acquisition costs / new customers. (4) Calculate your ARPU: total revenue / total active customers. (5) Calculate your gross margin percentage: (revenue - direct costs of delivery) / revenue. (6) Estimate your average customer lifespan in months — use your churn rate if you track it (lifespan = 1 / monthly churn rate). (7) Calculate your LTV: ARPU x gross margin x average lifespan. (8) Calculate your LTV:CAC ratio: LTV / CAC. (9) Calculate your payback period: CAC / (ARPU x gross margin). Write all nine numbers down. If your LTV:CAC is below 3:1, identify whether the problem is CAC (too expensive to acquire), margin (too expensive to serve), or retention (customers leave too quickly).

quiz.question: A company spends $15,000 on sales and marketing in a month and acquires 30 new customers. Each customer pays $100/month with 70% gross margin and stays for an average of 14 months. What is the LTV:CAC ratio?
quiz.explanation: CAC = $15,000 / 30 = $500. LTV = $100 x 0.70 x 14 = $980. LTV:CAC = $980 / $500 = 1.96x. This is below the 3:1 benchmark, indicating the model needs improvement — either reduce CAC, improve margins, or increase retention.

quiz.question: Why is calculating LTV on gross profit rather than revenue important?
options: It makes the LTV number look better for investors
Revenue does not account for the cost of delivering the product, so using it overstates the actual value each customer generates
Gross profit is easier to calculate than revenue
It is only important for hardware businesses, not SaaS
quiz.explanation: LTV should reflect the actual profit a customer generates, not just the revenue they pay. If a customer pays $100/month but it costs $40/month to serve them, the real value is $60/month. Using revenue instead of gross profit would overstate LTV by 67% and lead to poor decisions about acquisition spend.

quiz.question: A company's blended LTV:CAC ratio is 3.5x. When segmented, enterprise customers are at 8x and SMB customers are at 1.2x. What should the founder do?
options: Nothing — the blended ratio is healthy so the business is fine
Stop acquiring SMB customers entirely and go all-in on enterprise
Investigate the SMB segment to understand whether CAC, retention, or margins can be improved — and consider reallocating budget toward enterprise if not
Raise prices for all customers to improve the blended ratio
quiz.explanation: Blended metrics mask segment-level problems. The SMB segment at 1.2x is barely viable and may be destroying value. The founder should diagnose the root cause — is it high CAC, poor retention, or low margins? — and either fix the segment or shift resources to the segment that works.

perspectives.founder: Unit economics are the truth about whether your business model works. Revenue growth is vanity if every customer you acquire costs more than they are worth. You need to know your CAC, LTV, and payback period from memory — not because investors will ask (they will), but because these numbers tell you whether growing faster is a good idea or a faster path to failure. Check these monthly, segmented by channel.
perspectives.delegator: Have someone on your team — a finance lead, growth analyst, or even a sharp ops person — track the raw numbers monthly: acquisition spend by channel, new customers by channel, churn by cohort, and revenue per customer. They should prepare a one-page unit economics dashboard. Your job is to review it, ask questions, and make decisions. The data gathering should be systematic and automated where possible.
perspectives.investor: Investors scrutinise unit economics more than almost any other metric. They want to see a clear path to 3:1+ LTV:CAC with a payback period under 18 months. But more importantly, they want to see that you understand the drivers — that you know which segments are strong, which are weak, and what levers you are pulling to improve them. Founders who present only blended averages raise red flags. Founders who show cohort-level analysis and segmented economics demonstrate operational maturity.
perspectives.advisor: Focus on retention before acquisition. Most founders want to talk about reducing CAC, but the highest-leverage move is almost always improving retention. A 2-percentage-point improvement in monthly retention can increase LTV by 50% or more — and unlike CAC reduction, retention improvements compound over time. Start by measuring churn accurately (many founders undercount it), then identify the top three reasons customers leave, then fix the biggest one.
perspectives.scaler: At scale, small improvements in unit economics create massive value. If you have 10,000 customers and you improve monthly retention by 1 percentage point, that is hundreds of additional customer-months of revenue per year. If you reduce CAC by $50, that is $500,000 saved annually at 10,000 new customers per year. The operators who win at scale are the ones who built measurement discipline early — they know their unit economics by segment, by cohort, and by channel, and they optimise relentlessly.
